coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot nanoarchitectonics with high photoluminescence for efficient Cr ion sensors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Yi
Zhang, Xiao
Wang, Jianrong
Wang, Shuangyu
Yang, Ping
coredata.dc:description: To overcome that current carbon dots (CDs) can just detect chromium ions in a single valence state, bright N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were synthesized to respond Cr (III) and Cr (VI) at same time. Sample N, S-CDs with isothiocyanate functional groups were created for quick detection of Cr (III) and Cr (VI). The photoluminescence (PL) of N, S-CDs was adjusted from green to blue emission by changing the doping ratios of N and S. S doping improved the selective recognition ability of CDs to chromium ions. The linear ranges of N, S-CDs for Cr (III) and Cr (VI) are 0-14 and 0-140 µM, respectively, and the detection limits are 0.08 and 0.79 µM, respectively. Through the tests and calculation, the response mechanism of N, S-CDs to chromium ions was proposed. Enhanced PL form chelation resulted in a “turn-on” response mechanism for Cr (III) while the “turn-off” response to Cr (VI) was achieved through a combination of the inner filter effect and static quenching. Sample N, S-CDs were stable for detection of Cr (III) in neutral and weakly alkaline conditions (pH ∼ 7-9) and Cr (VI) in acidic to alkaline conditions (pH ∼ 2-12). In lake water, N, S-CDs maintain specific recognition of Cr (III) and Cr (VI), and the recovery of total chromium content was 100-103%. Due to low cost and simple synthesis method, the N, S-CDs can be used for on-field detection of both Cr (III) and Cr (VI).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Sensor
Cr ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cr (VI) and Cr (III) detection in lake water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Strategy for synthesis of N, S-CDs.

(a-c) TEM images of N, S-CDs. The inset in (a) shows the particle size distribution of N, S-CDs. (d-f) HRTEM images of N, S-CDs. The lattice fringes of CDs were observed in (e) and (f) corresponding to (004) and (020) facets, respectively. The inset in (f) shows fast Fourier transform pattern.
(a) FTIR spectra of N, S-CDs. (b) XRD patterns of N, S-CDs. (c) XPS survey spectra, (d) C 1s and (e) N 1s spectra of N-CDs and N, S-CDs. (f) S 2p spectrum of N, S-CDs.

(a) PL and excitation spectra of CDs with different sulfur doping amounts (solid lines are PL spectra; dashed lines are excitation spectra). (b) PL intensity evolution of CDs with S-doping amount. (c) PL and excitation spectra of N, S-CDs, MPD-CDs, TA-CDs and S-CDs. (d) PL spectra of N, S-CDs synthesized with temperature.

(a) Response of N-CDs to different metal ions. (b) Response of N, S-CDs to different metal ions. The insets in (a, b) show the pictures of CDs with different metal ions in an ion concentration of 1 mmol/L). (c) PL evolution of N, S-CDs with Cr (III) and Cr (VI). (d) PL spectra for N, S-CDs with Cr (III). (e) Relationship between Cr (III) concentration and the fluorescence quenching efficiency (I: fluorescence intensity of mixed solution, I0: fluorescence intensity). (f) Mechanism for Cr (III)-enhanced PL. (g) PL spectra of N, S-CDs with Cr (VI). (h) Relationship between Cr (VI) concentration and fluorescence quenching efficiency (I0/I-1). (i) PL intensity of N, S-CDs with temperature.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S-CDs in the absence and presence of Cr (III). (b) PL and excitation spectra of N, S-CDs and UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr (III) and Cr (VI). (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S-CDs in the absence and in the presence of Cr (VI).

(a) PL spectra of N, S-CDs at different pH values. (b) Integral area and FWHM of PL spectrum under different pH values. (c) PL evolution of N, S-CDs with Cr (III). (d) Brightness comparison of N, S-CDs with and without Cr (III) verses pH values. (e) PL evolution of N, S-CDs with Cr (VI). (f) Brightness comparison of N, S-CDs with and without Cr (VI) verses pH values.

(a) PL evolution of N, S-CDs to Cr (VI) and Cr (III) with different concentration. (b) Integral area of PL spectrum of N, S-CDs after adding Cr (VI) and Cr (III). (c) FWHM of PL spectrum of N, S-CDs with Cr (VI) and Cr (III).
Time constants τ1 and τ2, components B1 and B2, and average lifetime τavg of N, S-CDs with Cr (III) and Cr (VI) in different concentration.
Determination of Cr (III) and Cr (VI) in lake water.
Comparison of N, S-CDs with that reported in literature.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot nanoarchitectonics with high photoluminescence for efficient Cr ion sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, 250022, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Facult of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Warszawska 24 St. 31-155 Krakow, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Facult of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bFacult of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Warszawska 24 St. 31-155 Krakow, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To overcome that current carbon dots (CDs) can just detect chromium ions in a single valence state, bright N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were synthesized to respond Cr (III) and Cr (VI) at same time. Sample N, S-CDs with isothiocyanate functional groups were created for quick detection of Cr (III) and Cr (VI). The photoluminescence (PL) of N, S-CDs was adjusted from green to blue emission by changing the doping ratios of N and S. S doping improved the selective recognition ability of CDs to chromium ions. The linear ranges of N, S-CDs for Cr (III) and Cr (VI) are 0-14 and 0-140 µM, respectively, and the detection limits are 0.08 and 0.79 µM, respectively. Through the tests and calculation, the response mechanism of N, S-CDs to chromium ions was proposed. Enhanced PL form chelation resulted in a “turn-on” response mechanism for Cr (III) while the “turn-off” response to Cr (VI) was achieved through a combination of the inner filter effect and static quenching. Sample N, S-CDs were stable for detection of Cr (III) in neutral and weakly alkaline conditions (pH ∼ 7-9) and Cr (VI) in acidic to alkaline conditions (pH ∼ 2-12). In lake water, N, S-CDs maintain specific recognition of Cr (III) and Cr (VI), and the recovery of total chromium content was 100-103%. Due to low cost and simple synthesis method, the N, S-CDs can be used for on-field detection of both Cr (III) and Cr (VI).

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium as a “double-edged sword” metal element affects human health [1-4]. Previous studies focused on the detection and conversion of Cr (VI), so emissions were mostly converted from Cr (VI) to Cr (III). However, current studies have found that Cr (VI) generated by the oxidation of Cr (III) is gradually becoming the main source of Cr (VI) in soil and groundwater [5]. Therefore, the detection of Cr (III) is a seriously neglected but very necessary technical problem. Especially how to find a simple and reliable method to detect Cr (III) and Cr (VI) sensor platforms has become a big difficulty. Nowadays, few materials can simultaneously detect Cr (III) and Cr (VI) in water, in which high costs and complex synthesis methods limit the application [6-11]. Therefore, it is still a challenge to adopt cheap, simple, portable, low-toxicity, and sensitive methods for the determination of trace amounts of Cr (VI) and Cr (III).

Carbon dots (CDs) have become an emerging class of nano-luminescent materials due to their advantages of cheapness, nontoxicity, and high stability, which have a wide range of applications in the fields of sensing, photocatalysis, electrochemistry, anti-counterfeiting, and immunoassay [12-16]. Unfortunately, most of the CDs can only detect Cr in a single valence state and the detection mechanism has not been thoroughly investigated. Feng et al. achieved the detection of both valence ions by oxidizing Cr (III) to Cr (VI), but the intrinsic form of the CDs is still the “turn-off” detection of Cr (VI) [17]. Compared to 'turn-off' detection, 'turn-on' detection is less prone to false positives caused by various factors. The amplification of fluorescence intensity can be achieved by chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) or photogenerated electron transfer process, thus achieving excellent detection results [18-21].

The one-step direct synthesis of CDs with specific functionalities is a frequently disregarded yet highly significant research avenue [22-25]. In this paper, stable blue-emitting CDs (N, S-CDs) under acidic conditions were synthesized in one step using thiourea, terephthalic acid (TA), and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) as precursors. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of N, S-CDs was 27.6%, while the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PL spectrum was 69 nm. Sample N, S-CDs contain abundant functional groups such as -COOH/-NH2/-N=C=S. Among them, the -COOH/-NH2 can form a stable six-membered ring complex with Cr (III), leading to fluorescence enhancement. On the other hand, the highly electrophilic -N=C=S functional group can react with negatively charged Cr2O7 2− ions, resulting in static quenching. This combined with the internal filtration effect (IFE) enables the detection of Cr (VI) through a “turn-off” response. The total concentration of chromium ions could be detected by adding ascorbic acid (AA).

Thiourea, MPD (99.5%), ethanol, MnCl2·4H2O (99.0%), K2Cr2O7 (99.5%), ZnCl2 (98%), Na2MoO4·2H2O (99.0%), NiCl2·6H2O (99.0%), AlCl3·6H2O (97.0%), CuCl2 (99.99%), PbCl2 (99.5%) and AA (99.7%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The TA (99%), CdCl2·5/2H2O (98%), Cr (NO3)3·9H2O (99.99%), and SrCl2·6H2O (99.5%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Distilled water was used as a solvent to test the pH and ion sensitivity of CDs from a Millipore system (Merck 18.2 MΩ cm).

To endow highly bright and stabile CDs with tunable PL, N, S, and N/S-doped CDs were created using thiourea, TA, and MPD as precursors. Typically, N, S-CDs were synthesized as follows: 0.104 g thiourea, 0.007 g of TA, and 0.014 g of MPD were added to 10 mL of ethanol with sonication for 10 min, and then transferred to a 25 mL reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 180°C for 12 h. The amounts of CDs were prepared in a similar process, keeping the total mass of the raw materials at 0.125 g and the mass ratio of MPD and TA was kept at 2:1. Resulting samples were filtered off by a 0.22 μm filter. Sample N-doped CDs (N-CDs) were prepared with thiourea-free in the precursor, and the mass ratio of MPD to TA was kept as 2:1. Sample S-doped CDs (S-CDs) were prepared using a similar procedure using thiourea. Sample MPD-S-CDs represent the CDs prepared by MPD and thiourea as the precursor system. Sample TA-S-CDs represent the CDs prepared by TA and thiourea as the precursor system as illustrated in Table 1 .

The morphology observation was finished using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, US). Samples were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D8-ADVANCE, Bruker Corporation, Germany), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, the USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were analyzed by a VG Micro Tech ESCA 300 infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo Electron, the USA). The optical characteristics were studied by V-vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-4100, Japan), fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan), and Jobin Yvon IBH fluorescence lifetime analyzer (Horiba, Japan). The PLQY of samples were obtained according to eq. 1.
(1)
Φ
X
=
Φ
ST
[ Gra
d X
/ Gra
d ST
]
×
[
η
X
2
/
η
ST
2
]

Where Φ denotes the PLQY; Grad is the slope of the integrated area of the emission peak for the absorbance. The subscripts X and ST represent the CDs and reference sample Rhodamine 6G (PLQY= 98% in ethanol), respectively. η is the refractive index of the solvent.

In the experiment of selective detection of metal ions by CDs, a series of aqueous solutions of metal ions were configured (Mn2+, Zn2+, Mo6+, Ni2+, Al3+, Sr2+, K+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Au3+). Subsequently, the CDs solution was added to the metal ion aqueous solution, the concentration of CDs solution in water was 8 μL/10 mL, which was mixed evenly within 2 min for the fluorescence test. In the test for detecting the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the CDs and the concentration of chromium ions, and the detection limit of chromium ions was calculated according to the formula 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the fitted straight line. The fluorescence intensity of CDs versus ion concentration was fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation (eq. 2):
(2)
I /
I 0
=
K SV
×
C
Cr
( VI )

Where, I0 is the initial PL intensity of N, S-CDs in water, I is the PL intensity of N, S-CDs in the presence of chromium ions. KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant (slope of the concentration-dependent response).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr (VI) and Cr (III) detection in lake water
ce:section.ce:para: The competition between Cr (VI) and Cr (III) ions was performed as follows: 5 μL Cr (VI) solution (10 mM) was added into 2.5 mL S-CDs solution (3 μL /mL), and the change of fluorescence intensity was detected after mixing evenly. Then 5 μL Cr (III) solution (10 mM) was added and the change in fluorescence intensity was detected. The above steps were repeated until the concentrations of both Cr (VI) and Cr (III) reached 420 μM. 3 uL of N, S-CDs solution was added to 2.5 mL of real lake water samples and the fluorescence intensity was recorded, followed by the addition of a certain amount of Cr (VI) and Cr (III). Then 1 μL of 10 mM AA was added as a reducing agent to reduce Cr (VI) to Cr (III). The total chromium content could be calculated based on the fluorescence intensity after the addition of AA. The content of Cr (VI) reduced by AA can be calculated from the amount of change in fluorescence intensity before and after adding AA. The total chromium content minus the content of Cr (VI) is the content of Cr (III). The lake water was taken from the lake at our university. The water was used directly without treatment after sampling.

The preparation process of N, S-CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the presence of phenyl structures in MPD and TA provides conjugation for PL. Under high temperature and pressure conditions, MPD self-polymerize to form a fluorophore resembling phenzine-2,7-diamine, which is then cross-linked to form a polymer. Secondly, the condensation reaction between the amino group in MPD and the carboxyl group of TA facilitates amide bonds formation. In addition, thiourea contains unsaturated bonds as well as heteroatoms like nitrogen and sulfur, endowing it with excellent reactivity. Partial substitution of sulfur atoms for carbon atoms is involved in the formation of carbon core skeleton structure. The products derived from thiourea further reacted with other chemical bonds within the system to generate abundant functional groups.

As shown in Fig. 1a-1c , N, S-CDs revealed a spherical structure with uniform size. The diameter of N, S-CDs was 2-5 nm. The HRTEM image of a single N, S-CDs in Fig. 1d-1f revealed lattice fringes of 0.17 and 0.27 nm corresponding to the (004) and (020) planes, respectively [26]. Fig. 1f shows that a larger N, S-CD with a diameter of 7.8 nm. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of a single N, S-CDs in the inset in Fig. 1f indicates that the N, S-CDs are almost defect-free graphene single crystals. These findings highlight the clear and complete lattice phase, high degree of graphitization, and efficient electron transport exhibited by the N, S-CDs.

Fig. 2 a illustrates that differences in thiourea content in the precursors significantly affect the FTIR spectra of the CDs. The absorption peak at 3450 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group, while the peaks at 3162 and 846 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the amide and the out-of-plane swinging vibration peaks, respectively [27,28]. The peak at 1620 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C=O in the amide [29]. The vibrational peaks of C=O were gradually enhanced with the increase in thiourea proportion in the precursor, which was attributed to the fact that the pyrolysis product of thiourea, carbodiimide, could activate the carboxyl group and contribute to the generation of amides and esters. The peak of 1662 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C=N; graphite N gradually increased with thiourea doping, suggesting that the nitrogen from thiourea also participated in carbon skeleton formation [30]. The peak at 1400 cm−1 was from the stretching vibration of C=S [29]. A significant C-S bond can be produced by 7% sulfur doping, as evidenced by the characteristic peak of C-S around 1163 cm−1.

The peaks in 2065 and 1105 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibrations of -N=C=S, respectively [31,32]. The gradual enhancement of these two peaks with thiourea suggests that the introduction of thiourea increases the isothiocyanate functional groups in the CDs, which changes the structural and optical properties of the CDs. The XRD spectrum of Fig. 2b illustrates that N, S-CDs are mainly amorphous carbon structures, and the peak at 23.8° corresponds to the (002) plane of graphene carbon material [29].

Fig. 2c shows the XPS spectra of samples. Nitrogen and sulfur were doped in N, S-CDs. The C 1s spectra in Fig. 2d were fitted into 284.8 eV for C-C or C=C, 286.2 eV for C-O or C-N, and 288.9 eV for O-C=O [32,33]. The binding energies of carbon and heteroatoms in N, S-CDs were shifted towards low energies, suggesting that the electron transfer between carbon and doped elements is enhanced and the density of the electron cloud around the carbon atoms is reduced, which has a weaker effect on the binding of the photoelectrons. The peak at 285.2 eV representing sp3 hybrid carbon is increased due to the inclusion of the peak of C-S bonding. The binding energy at 288.3 eV may contain both O-C=O and C=N chemical bonding, suggesting that the CDs may have formed more conjugated structures after the introduction of thiourea. The spectrum of the nitrogen of the N-CDs can be convolved into pyridine nitrogen (399.2 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (399.8 eV), and graphite nitrogen (400.8 eV) (Fig. 2e) [34,35]. The binding energies of N, S-CDs are shifted to the low-energy direction, and the contents of pyrrole nitrogen and graphite nitrogen are increased, which indicates that the introduction of thiourea can increase the binding stability of carbon atoms and nitrogen atoms in the CDs, forming a tighter doping structure. The peaks at 162.3 and 163.5 eV in Fig. 2f represent S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 in C-S bonds, respectively [36,37]; the peaks at 163.9 and 165.1 eV with higher binding energies can be attributed to S 2p3/2 and S2p1/2 in unsaturated C=S in compounds containing nitrogen and sulfur [31,38].

Sample N-CDs with a PLQY of 34.0% were synthesized using TA and MPD as indicated in Fig. 3 a and Table 2 . The carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl functional groups on their surface ensure their hydrophilicity [39,40]. Fig. 3a and 3b show that the introduction of thiourea can significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of blue light emitted from the CDs, which is because the thiourea as a precursor can provide a shorter carbon chain and a high content of amino groups. Because of sulfur with a large radius and strong electronegativity compared with the carbon atom, N, S-CDs revealed enhanced short wavelength PL [41]. In contrast, a high percentage of thiourea in the precursor led to a significant decrease in PLQYs because of the low percentage of benzene ring precursors.

Fig. 3c shows the effect of TA and MPD on PL properties. The extremely low PL intensity of S-CDs synthesized by thiourea is ascribed to the lack of sp2-structured carbon in the precursor. The introduced TA can enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs (TA-S-CDs) to some extent. After adding MPD, the PL intensity was increased drastically (MPD-CDs) although the FWHM was large, suggesting that the introduction of amino groups has made the CDs structure more complex and diverse. The co-introduction of TA and MPD produces a synergistic effect that can further enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Fig. 3d shows the effect of temperature. In the case of less than 180°C, CDs were unable, resulting in low PLQYs and broad FWHM of PL spectra. In contrast, a high temperature lead to excessive carbonization of the CDs, which in turn reduced the amount of effective fluorescent functional groups. The PL peaks of the CDs prepared at 180°C and 220°C overlapped, indicating that CDs were stable at more than 180°C.

Fig. 4 a and 4b demonstrate the fluorescence responsiveness of N, S-CDs and N-CDs to different metal ions in water. N, S-CDs revealed selective for Cr (VI). Interestingly, N, S-CDs revealed “turn-off” and “turn-on” modes for Cr (VI) and Cr (III), respectively. Fig. 4c shows that the PL of N, S-CDs was quenched by 1 mM Cr (VI) solution while the PL was enhanced by 1 mM Cr (III) solution. Fig. 4d shows that the PL peaks of N, S-CDs gradually red-shifted and their FWHM was broadened with the concentration of Cr (III). N, S-CDs revealed a linear response in the range of 0 ∼ 14 μM for Cr (III) and a detection limit of 0.08 μM (Fig. 4e). The amino and carboxyl groups in the precursor are retained on the surface of the N, S-CDs and can be complex with Cr (III) to form a six-membered ring structure, resulting in CHEF (Fig. 4f). Cr (VI) quenched the PL of N, S-CDs as shown in Fig. 4g with a linear range of 0-140 μM and a detection limit of 0.79 μM (Fig. 4h). In addition, the CDs revealed high stability with temperature (Fig. 4i).

To investigate enhanced fluorescence mechanism between Cr (III) and N, S-CDs, the absorption spectra of N, S-CDs mixed solutions with varying concentrations of Cr (III) were tested. As depicted in Fig. 5 a. the peak at 232 nm was attributed to the π-π* leaps of C=C/C=N bonds, while the absorption peak at 345 nm came from the n-π* leaps of n orbitals of lone pairs of electrons in N/O heteroatoms at the edges of the carbon lattice with the unsaturated bonds such as -N=C=S [42,43]. As the concentration of Cr (III) was elevated, the absorption at 232 nm gradually diminished and the intensity of the absorption peak at 345 nm undergone a red shift by approximately 10 nm. This indicates that Cr (III) coordinates with carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of N, S-CDs to form a stable six-membered ring rigid structure, which stimulates the π-electron delocalization effect, reducing the energy required for the electron transition [7,44]. This phenomenon suggests that the sensitive detection of trace Cr (III) is triggered by the "turn-on" of CHEF.

Different from the aqueous solution of Cr (III), Cr (VI) solutions revealed two strong absorption peaks at about 250-400 nm. The absorption peak at 365 nm overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CDs (Fig. 5b), suggesting an IFE occurred. The IFE did not belong to static or dynamic quenching and has no effect on the absorption peaks and fluorescence lifetime of CDs. However, upon addition of Cr(VI), slight changes are observed in the absorption peaks of N, S-CDs as depicted in Fig. 5c, indicating potential generation of new substances and suggesting a possible presence of static quenching within this system. Furthermore, the overlap of the absorption spectra of Cr(VI) with the emission spectra of N, S-CDs (Blue shading in Fig. 5b) implies a potential Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) quenching mechanism. However, it should be noted that fluorescence lifetime measurements for N, S-CDs remain nearly unchanged after introduction of Cr(VI) (Table 3 ), confirming only static quenching and IFE while excluding FRET quenching. Subsequently, the KSV (Stern-Volmer quenching constant) was calculated from the Stern-Volmer equation (eq. 2) to be 3.76 × 103 mol (see Fig. 4h for the fitted plot). The associated kq (quenching rate constant) was calculated from eq. 3.
(3)
K
S V
=
k q
τ 0

Where, τ0 is the average lifetime of N, S-CD. The kq was 6.41 × 1011 L mol−1s−1, which was much larger than the typical diffusion-controlled quenching rate (10 × 103 L mol−1s−1) [45]. It was also larger than the dynamic quenching constant (2 × 1010 L mol−1s−1). In summary, the fluorescence quenching of N, S-CD by Cr (VI) was not only due to the IFE but also to the ligand bonding of Cr(VI) to functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl groups and amines) on N, S-CD to form nonfluorescent ground-state complex (static quenching effect) [46].

In previous studies on the detection of chromium ions by CDs, the effect of the pH value on the detection ability of CDs is overlooked. This is because the process of reducing Cr (VI) to Cr (III) and the detection of Cr (VI) are usually carried out under acidic conditions [47,48]. However, a low pH environment shields the negatively charged groups, which reduces the affinity between the functional groups of the CDs and the metal ions, therefore affecting their fluorescence response [49]. Therefore, it is important to design CDs that can maintain a sensitive response to chromium ions over a wide pH range.

The stability of N, S-CDs was first tested in aqueous solutions at different pH values and the results are shown in Fig. 6 a and 6b. The fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs was basically unchanged in acidic to weakly alkaline environments (pH ∼1-10). When the pH was more than 10, the PL gradually decreased and blue-shifted, indicating that the alkalinity of the solution significantly affects the protonation degree of the functional groups on the surface of the N, S-CDs, which in turn alters their emission wavelengths. The FWHM of the PL spectrum of N, S-CDs increased sharply, which also confirmed that its homogeneity was destroyed by the alkaline solution. Therefore, N, S-CDs can also be used for the detection of acidity and weak alkalinity of aqueous solutions.

An intriguing phenomenon can be observed from Fig. 6c and 6d, that N, S-CDs exhibit high sensitivity to Cr (III) in water with a pH range of neutral to weakly alkaline (pH∼7-9). However, the impact of Cr (III) is minimal in other pH solutions. This phenomenon corroborates the speculation mentioned before that the specific recognition of Cr (III) by N, S-CDs comes from the -NH2 and -COOH functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs. Acidic environments would protonate -NH2 to -NH3 +, and the positively charged -NH3 + would repel the Cr (III) charge and thus move away from each other. In a strongly alkaline environment, the situation is more complicated. Firstly, Cr (OH)3 has a low solubility in solution, thus preventing the effective interaction of CDs with Cr (III) [49]. Secondly, Cr (OH)3 is easily oxidized to Cr (VI) by oxygen in the air under alkaline conditions, thus affecting its detection [50]. In addition, the deprotonation process of functional groups on the surface of the CDs affected the luminescent groups of the CDs.

The addition of Cr (VI) resulted in a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs at all pH values, as shown in Fig. 6e and 6f. Especially in neutral to weakly alkaline environments, the effect of Cr (VI) on the quenching of CDs was very obvious. In acidic environments, isothiocyanate is prone to decomposition, thus affecting the sensitivity of N, S-CDs to Cr (VI) detection partially. However, overall, the specific recognition of Cr (VI) by N, S-CDs is applicable in the range of pH∼2-12 due to the high pH stability of N, S-CDs.

Fig. 7 a and 7b show the PL quenching of Cr (VI) on N, S-CDs with Cr (III) concentration. The fluorescence quenching effect of Cr (VI) on N, S-CDs increased for high Cr (III) concentration. Fig. 7c shows that the FWHM of PL spectra was increased with the total concentration of chromium ions, which proves that the homogeneity of the system is decreasing.

Subsequently, sample N, S-CDs were tested for the detection of Cr (III) and Cr (VI) in real samples, and the results are shown in Table 4 . Sample N, S-CDs showed excellent corresponding abilities for both Cr (VI) and Cr (III) in a single valence state. The recovery was in the range of 98-102%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3%. AA was introduced as a reducing agent for the determination of total chromium content in the mixed Cr (VI) and Cr (III) samples. Although the method was less accurate in quantifying chromium ions in different valence states compared to solutions of chromium ions in a single valence state, the recoveries of total chromium content were in the range of 100-103% with an RSD of less than 2%, which is extremely accurate. Some sites within N, S-CDs simultaneously affect both Cr (III) and Cr (VI), leading to reduced accuracy in detecting their valence states. Therefore, the concentration of chromium ions in the two valence states can be further accurately quantified by combining other means [10,11].

The effectiveness of N, S-CDs for detecting chromium ions was compared with other reported CDs (Table 5 ). There are limited studies on the ability of CDs to detect Cr (III), and most of them require the oxidantion of Cr (III) to Cr (VI) before fluorescence quenching [17]. False positive outcomes can occur with fluorescence “turn off” detection due to external quenchers or environmental factors. Hence, employing a fluorescence “turn-on” approach is an effective solution. The sensitivity of N, S-CD to Cr (VI) is slightly reduced, with a detection limit of 0.79 µM. However, sample N, S-CD exhibits a sharp responsiveness in the pH range of 2-12, indicating its great potential for application. The statistics indicate that N-CDs exhibit high sensitivity and a low detection limit, thereby demonstrating significant potential for the detection of two-valence chromium ions. In summary, the present work can achieve specific recognition and detection of Cr (III) and Cr (VI) using only one kinds of CDs, and the corresponding differentiation of the valence states of the two chromium ions is achieved by the combination of CHEF and IFE. According to our investigation, the CDs reported in this work are the first ones to achieve the detection of Cr (VI) and Cr (III) by different principles.

The detection of Cr (VI) over a wide pH range and the detection of its reduction products Cr (III) is an issue that is often neglected but very necessary in practical applications. In this work, N, S co-doped blue-emitting CDs (N, S-CDs) with isothiocyanate functional groups and high PLQY of 27.6% were synthesized from inexpensive precursors which can recognize Cr (VI) and quenching fluorescence by IFE and electron transfer with a detection limit as 0.79 mM and a linear range of 0-140 μM, and maintain their sensitivity over a wide pH range (pH∼2-12). Sample N, S-CDs can also achieve fluorescence “turn-on” detection of Cr (III) by CHEF with a detection limit as 0.08 mM and a linear range of 0-14 μM. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first CDs that can rapidly detect two valence states of chromium ions with 100-103% recoveries of total chromium content. This work has promising potential applications in the detection of Cr (VI) and Cr (III) in industrial or domestic sewage, selective chelating agent of chromium ions, and monitoring of valence transformation of chromium ions. Furthermore, the accuracy of N, S-CDs for the detection of each valence state of chromium ions in mixed solutions of Cr (III) and Cr (VI) needs to be further improved.

Yi Tian: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiao Zhang: Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jianrong Wang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Shuangyu Wang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Ping Yang: Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported partly by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (grant no. 51972145), Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau, China (No. 2021GXRC109).
